flagged_rows.stem: Why does the presence of a nearly universal genetic code across different organisms support the concept of a common evolutionary origin?
flagged_rows.correct_answer: Because it indicates deep conservation of fundamental molecular processes inherited from a shared ancestor
distractors: Because it shows that different species independently evolved the same genetic code by chance
Because it means all organisms have identical cellular structures and functions
Because it suggests that the genetic code changes rapidly over evolutionary time

flagged_rows.issue: Generated question does not stay aligned with the target learning objective.
flagged_rows.stem: Why is understanding the history of biochemical innovation important for comprehending the evolution of life on Earth?
flagged_rows.correct_answer: Because biochemical innovations like photosynthesis have fundamentally shaped the development and complexity of life on our planet
distractors: Because biochemical innovations primarily affect individual organism behaviors rather than evolutionary history
Because biochemical changes occur independently from evolutionary processes and do not influence life development
Because biochemical innovation is only relevant to the study of chemistry, not biology or evolution

flagged_rows.issue: Generated question does not stay aligned with the target learning objective.
flagged_rows.stem: Which theistic view integrates the idea that God established natural laws which then allowed evolutionary processes to proceed without further direct intervention?
flagged_rows.correct_answer: Theistic evolution, where God invented the rules and let natural processes run
distractors: Young earth creationism, which holds species were created separately about 6000 years ago
Gap creationism, which believes the Earth is old but biodiversity appeared suddenly
Progressive creationism, involving mutations and selection combined with occasional divine interventions

flagged_rows.issue: Generated question does not stay aligned with the target learning objective.
flagged_rows.stem: How does the molecular clock method estimate the time of a common ancestor between species?
flagged_rows.correct_answer: By measuring genetic differences and assuming a relatively constant rate of evolution to approximate divergence time
distractors: By comparing physical features and assuming species with similar traits share a recent common ancestor
By dating the age of fossils directly associated with each species to determine when they coexisted
By calculating the rate of environmental change and correlating it with species divergence times

flagged_rows.stem: Why are human-based in vitro models increasingly preferred over animal models in some areas of biomedical testing?
flagged_rows.correct_answer: Because they can better replicate human physiology and avoid species differences that limit animal model relevance
distractors: Because animal models are no longer legally permitted for any type of biomedical testing
Because in vitro models are always more cost-effective and easier to use than animal models
Because animal models never share any genetic or physiological similarities with humans

flagged_rows.stem: Why are electrical sparks used in laboratory simulations of early Earth conditions when studying the origins of life?
flagged_rows.correct_answer: Because electrical sparks simulate lightning which provides energy to drive chemical reactions forming organic molecules
distractors: Because electrical sparks sterilize the experimental setup to avoid contamination

flagged_rows.stem: Why is the concept of homologous traits important in understanding the Tree of Life?
flagged_rows.correct_answer: Because homologous traits indicate shared ancestry by showing features inherited from a common ancestor
distractors: Because homologous traits are characteristics that evolve independently in unrelated species due to similar environments
Because homologous traits identify traits that appear only in the latest branches of the Tree of Life
Because homologous traits emphasize differences that are more important than common ancestry in classification
